title: No-Sew Cayden Nailhead Bar Stool
summary: A bar-height stool with double-beveled splayed legs and an upholstered, nailhead-trimmed seat — no sewing required.
description:
The trick to this stool is the legs, not the upholstery. They're cut on a 5° double bevel — the miter saw tilted and angled at the same time — so each leg leans out on two axes at once. That's what gives the stool its splayed, mid-century stance instead of looking like a plain box with sticks under it.

Get the legs right and everything else is straightforward: a plywood or 2x12 seat blank wrapped in foam and fabric, trimmed with a ring of decorative nails instead of a sewn seam — hence "no-sew." The stretchers are the other place people go wrong, because their height is measured up from the floor, not up the angled leg, so you can't just mark a distance along the leg and call it done. Dry-fit the base, prop the stretcher on scrap to get the height right, then commit.
timeLabel: 3.5–5 hrs, plus time for the upholstery glue to set

tools.note: Must tilt AND angle at once for the 5° double-bevel leg cuts — a compound miter saw, not a basic chop saw.
For stretching and securing the fabric before the nailhead trim goes on.
Check the base sits flat with no rock before you upholster — much easier to fix now than after.

cutList.note: Wrapped in foam and fabric; this dimension is the finished blank before upholstery bulk.
Ends cut parallel at a 5° double bevel — tilt and miter set together, same setting for every cut.
Ends NOT parallel, each cut at 5° off square.
Ends NOT parallel, each cut at 5° off square.
One end cut at 5° off square; small spacer blocks under the seat trim.

steps.title: Cut all four legs on one saw setting
steps.body:
Set the miter saw for a 5° double bevel — the blade both tilted 5° and angled 5° at the same time. Square off the end of the 2x2, then without rotating the board, slide down 27-1/2" and cut. That's one leg. Slide down another 27-1/2" and cut again for the next.

Don't change the saw setting between legs — that's what keeps all four identical, and identical legs are the whole game here. Once you have four, stand them up in position like they'll sit on the stool and mark the inside corner of each one. It's genuinely hard to tell which way a splayed leg angles once it's off the saw, and you do not want to find out you glued one in backward after assembly.

steps.title: Attach the stretchers
steps.body:
This build is designed around a pocket hole jig; if you don't have one, countersunk screws work fine. Drill pocket holes in the ends of the four stretchers.

The tricky part: stretcher height is measured up from the FLOOR, not along the angled leg. Stand the legs against a flat, square reference edge, prop the stretcher on a scrap of 3/8" plywood or similar, and adjust the shim until the stretcher sits at the correct height off the floor. Then attach. This is not a step to rush — get it wrong and the stool sits crooked.

steps.title: Join the two leg assemblies
steps.body: With front and back stretcher pairs built, join the two sides into one base — this is where the project stops being flat and becomes three-dimensional, so go slow and dry-fit before you commit glue. Check the base sits flat with no rock; shim or trim a leg now if it doesn't.

steps.title: Attach the seat
steps.body: Mark the stretcher locations on the underside of the seat blank so you know exactly where to drive screws once it's flipped over, then attach the seat to the base from underneath.

steps.title: Pad and cover the seat — no sewing
steps.body:
Layer the foam pad on top of the seat blank, then a layer of batting over that. Pull the fabric or faux leather taut over both layers and staple it to the underside of the seat, working from the center of each side outward and rotating so tension stays even — the same technique as stapling a canvas, no needle involved.

Trim the excess fabric close to the staples, then press decorative nails around the perimeter to cover the staple line and finish the edge. Space them evenly — mark the spacing in pencil first if you want them dead consistent.

images.alt: Bar-height stool with splayed legs and a nailhead-trimmed upholstered seat
